ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper addresses a well-studied problem (sentiment analysis on app reviews and tweets) and the described contribution is two supervised-learning-based tools (App2Check and Tweet2Check) trained on existing types of datasets. The methodology appears to reuse standard supervised approaches and application-specific training rather than introducing a novel algorithmic approach, and key engine details are withheld due to non-disclosure, limiting evidence of methodological innovation. While the authors report strong empirical performance against many baselines, that alone—without novel methods or disclosed techniques—suggests limited originality beyond combining and tuning existing techniques for these domains.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper presents broad empirical comparisons across multiple app-review and tweet benchmarks (separate Italian/English test sets, large 10k app sets, and tweet corpora) and compares against 19 research tools plus SentiStrength, which supports the main performance claims. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, engine details and hyperparameters are withheld due to non-disclosure, and code is not mentioned, limiting reproducibility. Related work coverage is reported as adequate, and some limitations are noted (e.g., non-disclosure and dataset class coverage), but failure modes and deeper negative-result analysis are sparse. Overall the experiments are adequate in scope but lack important analyses and reproducibility detail.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and reports comparisons against a broad set of baselines (19 research tools via iFeel plus SentiStrength) on multiple held-out test sets for apps and tweets, which supports the main empirical claims. However, critical methodological details are missing or restricted (engine internals not disclosed, no hyperparameters or code reported), and the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or full dataset/class-distribution handling (notably the English tweet set lacks the neutral class), which limits reproducibility and weakens confidence in the superiority claims. Overall the experimental setup and baselines appear reasonable, but the undocumented model details and missing evaluation rigour justify a cautious rating.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (App2Check and Tweet2Check and their claims). Related work coverage is adequate and the writing quality is described as adequate. However, important presentation gaps remain: engine details are withheld due to non-disclosure and hyperparameters/code are not reported, which reduces clarity and reproducibility despite otherwise clear organization.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (sentiment analysis for app reviews and tweets) is squarely within NLP/ML scope and the paper evaluates on substantial multilingual test sets (A-ita/A-eng, B-ita/B-eng, tweet benchmarks) using standard metrics (Macro-F1, per-class F1) and extensive baselines (19 research tools + SentiStrength). However, the methods are described only at a high level (supervised models) and key details are withheld due to non-disclosure, with code and hyperparameters not reported, which undermines reproducibility and the level of methodological contribution typically expected at top-tier venues. Given strong empirical evaluation but limited transparency and unclear novelty, the fit is borderline appropriate.

IMPACT.justification: The paper targets a practically important problem—sentiment quantification for app reviews and tweets—and reports that App2Check and Tweet2Check outperform 19 state-of-the-art baselines (and SentiStrength for Italian) across multiple sizable test sets in both Italian and English, which suggests useful empirical gains and immediate practitioner value (web services and demo provided). However, the engine details are undisclosed, code and hyperparameters are not reported, and the English tweet set lacks a neutral class, all of which limit reproducibility and the likelihood of this work reshaping research directions. Overall, the contribution is practically valuable and likely to be used by practitioners but is not positioned to be field-changing for researchers.

REPLICABILITY.justification: Key model details are withheld due to non-disclosure restrictions and hyperparameters are not reported, so training/configuration cannot be reproduced. The data description lists test sets (sizes, apps, and cited tweet sources) but availability and preprocessing are not clearly specified. Code is not mentioned and engine internals/NLP pipeline are undisclosed, while evaluation metrics are stated (MF1, Acc, class F1) but that alone is insufficient to replicate the experiments.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of baselines (19 state-of-the-art research tools via the iFeel platform and SentiStrength for Italian) and evaluates on multiple relevant test sets (app review sets and Araújo/SemEval tweet benchmarks) using standard metrics (macro F1, accuracy, per-class F1), which indicates generally fair and appropriate comparisons. However, key methodological details are withheld (engine details not disclosed, hyperparameters and code not reported), and the summary does not indicate that the authors acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably or provide a clear analysis of differences versus baselines. These omissions are minor but limit the depth and reproducibility of the comparison.

main_claims: We present App2Check and Tweet2Check, two supervised learning-based tools for sentiment quantification specialized on app reviews and tweets.
The tools provide sentiment polarity quantification from 1 to 5 (mapped to negative/neutral/positive according to SemEval conventions).
App2Check outperforms state-of-the-art research competitors on app reviews.
Tweet2Check outperforms state-of-the-art research competitors on tweets in both Italian and English.
The tools are available as web services and a user-friendly web application, and benchmarks and a demo are provided with the paper.
_summary.methodology: App2Check and Tweet2Check implement supervised learning techniques trained on a variety of language domains to create two predictive models specialized for app reviews and tweets; additional NLP algorithms (e.g., topic/named-entity extraction) are applied, but further engine details are not disclosed due to non-disclosure restrictions.
datasets: Test set A-ita (1000 Italian comments from Candy Crush Saga, manually annotated 1-5)
Test set A-eng (1000 English comments from Candy Crush Saga, manually annotated 1-5)
Test set B-ita (10,000 comments: Angry Birds, Banco Posta, Facebook, Fruit Ninja, Gmail, Mobile Banking Unicredit, My Vodafone, PayPal, Twitter, Whatsapp — 1000 per app)
Test set B-eng (10,000 comments: Candy Crush Soda Saga, Chase Mobile, Clash Of Clans, Facebook Messenger, Gmail, Instagram, My Verizon, PayPal, Snapchat, Wells Fargo — 1000 per app)
Tweet benchmark (3899 Italian tweets from Araújo et al., 2016 / Sem, 2016)
Tweet benchmark (1000 English tweets randomly selected from the English dataset in Araújo et al., 2016 / Sem, 2016)
baselines: 19 state-of-the-art research tools run via the iFeel platform
limitations: It is not possible to give more details on the engine due to non-disclosure restrictions.
The English tweet dataset used does not contain the neutral class.
According to SentiStrength authors, SentiStrength has human-level accuracy for short social web texts in English except political texts.
